ask-first.summary: ALWAYS confirm API version and modeling types BEFORE writing any Cameo script. SysMLv1 vs SysMLv2 vs UAF vs plain UML have different profile names, different stereotype FQNs, and different factories — guessing wrong burns a whole iteration.
do: At the start of each session, call cameo_profile_status. If no profile is active OR modelingTypes is empty, STOP and ask the user: 'Which API version? (e.g. 26xR1) Which modeling types are enabled on the target project? (UML / SysMLv1 / SysMLv2 / UAF / KerML)'.
If the user answers with a new environment, call cameo_profile_add with the answers and activate:true.
If the user says 'SysMLv1', never suggest com.dassault_systemes.modeler.sysml.* (that is SysMLv2). Use com.nomagic.magicdraw.sysml.util.SysMLProfile.
If the user says 'SysMLv2' or 'KerML', use the com.dassault_systemes.modeler.* packages; com.nomagic.magicdraw.sysml.* is the v1 API.
Re-confirm when the user switches projects — the active profile may no longer match the new project's profiles.
dont: Don't assume 'SysML' means SysMLv1 or SysMLv2. Ask.
Don't guess the version from the defaults — the default profile is whatever was last activated, which may be stale.
Don't fall back to plain UML imports when the user said SysMLv1 — the SysML profile is what they almost certainly want.
ask-first.source: 2026-04-22 user requirement: agent must ask up front when info is missing

sessions.summary: Every model mutation must be inside a SessionManager session; cancel on error.
do: Use SessionManager.getInstance().createSession(project, 'descriptive label') before any mutating call.
Call closeSession(project) on success to commit.
Call cancelSession(project) inside a catch block to roll back; then rethrow.
Keep sessions short — one logical change per session.
dont: Never leave a session open on exception — always cancel or close.
Don't nest sessions unless you really mean to; SessionManager is process-wide state.
Don't mutate the model outside a session — some calls silently no-op, others throw.

error-reporting.summary: Three channels: log4j for plugin/diagnostic traces, GUILog for user-visible dialogs, and a diagnostic file under logs/ so Claude can read failures.
do: Use the SysMLv2Logger wrapper (see 'console-logger-class' snippet) to route info/debug to log4j only and warn/error to both log4j and GUILog.
Use Application.getInstance().getGUILog().showError/showMessage/showQuestion for user dialogs only.
Use MDLog.getGeneralLog() or log4j directly for plugin diagnostic output.
Always pass the Throwable to the (msg, Throwable) overload — never call error(t.getMessage()) alone.
Also write to logs/ under the project so Claude Code can inspect failures on the next iteration (per project CLAUDE.md).
dont: Never swallow an exception — no empty catch blocks, no 'log.error(t.getMessage())' without the throwable.
Don't spam GUILog with info/debug chatter; reserve it for warnings, errors, and user prompts.

finder.summary: Prefer Finder.byQualifiedName and Finder.byTypeRecursively over hand-rolled owner traversal.
do: Finder.byQualifiedName().find(project, 'A::B::C', ExpectedType.class) when you know the model path.
Finder.byTypeRecursively().find(owner, typeArray, includeOwner) when scanning a subtree.
Always null-check the result — Finder returns null/empty, not throws, when nothing matches.
dont: Don't walk owner.getOwnedElement() recursively by hand — it's slow, error-prone, and misses some containment paths.

no-fast-strings.summary: Forbidden: Groovy GStrings (double-quoted with $ interpolation) at any Cameo API boundary. They are GStringImpl, not java.lang.String, and Cameo APIs that check 'instanceof String' silently misbehave.
do: Use single-quoted strings: 'hello ' + name.
Use '+' concatenation for dynamic content passed to Cameo APIs.
If you must build a GString first, call .toString() before handing it to Cameo: someCameoApi.setName(gstr.toString()).
For log messages that stay inside Groovy (log4j, System.out), GStrings are fine — the rule is about crossing into Cameo Java code.
dont: Don't write element.setName("Attr_${i}") — that's a GString.
Don't assume GStringImpl will auto-coerce — sometimes it does, but the failures are subtle and version-dependent.
Don't try to fix it with an 'as String' cast at the last minute — convert at the source.
no-fast-strings.source: user convention; Groovy docs on GStringImpl vs String

no-system-exit.summary: System.exit() kills MagicDraw's JVM and can corrupt unsaved work. Never call it from a script or plugin.
do: Return from the script body to end a run.
Call dispose() or setVisible(false) on JFrames/JDialogs.
Unregister listeners and close resources in a finally block.
For command-line plugins, return a non-zero byte from CommandLineAction.execute — do NOT call System.exit.
dont: Never call System.exit at any exit code.
Don't call Runtime.getRuntime().halt either — same problem, worse.
Don't kill the Java process from outside unless you logged the PID and know it's yours (per project CLAUDE.md).

headless.summary: No built-in isHeadless() in the Cameo API. Detect by checking for a null Application or null current Project, or by structuring code as a CommandLineAction.
do: Check Application.getInstance() != null && Application.getInstance().getProject() != null before touching GUI code paths.
For CLI-style tools, implement com.nomagic.magicdraw.commandline.CommandLineAction and return a status byte.
Degrade gracefully: in headless mode, operate on test fixtures; in interactive mode, use the current project.
dont: Don't call Application.getInstance().getMainFrame() without a headless guard — it's null in CLI mode.
Don't open a JDialog or Swing component in headless mode — it throws HeadlessException.

rest-harness.summary: When using the REST test harness in MagicDraw, always clean up before relaunching — stale classloaders cache compiled Groovy and hide fixes.
do: Before launching a new test, send a clean-shutdown REST command to the prior run to release its classloader and dispose GUI.
Ensure your script is tolerant of being relaunched — idempotent setup, no static singletons that assume first-launch semantics.
Log PID + timestamp + code-version to logs/ so you always know which process you started (per CLAUDE.md).
dont: Don't restart a test without stopping the prior one — stale classes stick around and produce confusing results.
Don't kill a JVM PID you didn't start yourself.

collections-are-live.summary: Many Cameo API getters return live, model-backed collections. Mutating while iterating causes ConcurrentModificationException or silent data loss.
do: Copy before mutating: new ArrayList<>(elem.getOwnedElement()).each { ... }.
Or collect the mutations first (a List of closures/records) and apply them after iteration.
dont: Don't mutate the element's owned collection inside a for/each loop over the same collection.
Don't assume a copy — always check the Javadoc; many getters are documented as 'live view'.

association-ends.summary: UML Association has no slots; the real ends live in getEnd(). end[0] is source, end[1] is target. Always confirm multiplicity and type before dereferencing.
do: Read ends as: def ends = assoc.getEnd(); def source = ends[0]; def target = ends[1].
Check instanceof/respondsTo before casting — associations can end on multiple metaclasses.
Inspect multiplicity via end.getLower() / end.getUpper() before assuming cardinality.
dont: Don't look for association data in slots — there aren't any.
Don't assume end[0] is the 'owning' side; orientation follows the model, not the ordering.

tdd-loop.summary: Test-first iterative flow: commit → write test → implement → run tests + REST-harness → inspect diagnostic logs → repeat. Max 10 red cycles before escalation.
do: Commit current state BEFORE making a code change so rollback is cheap.
Write or update the test that captures the new expectation FIRST.
After each change: run unit + integration tests AND the REST-harness run.
Read diagnostic log files (not just console) to confirm pass/fail.
Commit with a message that explains *why*, not just *what*.
dont: Don't create a new file just because you 'think you fixed something' — edit in place.
Don't start a new REST-harness test without cleanly stopping the prior one — stale class cache.
Don't loop more than 10 red cycles without asking the user for guidance.

console-logger.summary: Use the SysMLv2Logger wrapper for all script logging — it routes info/debug to log4j only and mirrors warn/error to the MagicDraw GUI console.
do: Define SysMLv2Logger once (see 'console-logger-class' snippet) and load it via 'script-load-groovy'.
Instantiate per script: def logger = LoggerClass.newInstance('MyFeatureName').
Always call logger.error(msg, throwable) — the (msg, Throwable) overload preserves the stack trace in both log4j and GUILog.
dont: Don't sprinkle Application.getInstance().getGUILog().log(...) calls throughout a script — centralize via the logger.
Don't log every info at GUILog — it clutters the console and hides real problems.

dedicated-log-file.summary: For scripts launched interactively inside Cameo (not via batch CLI), write to a dedicated log file that is CLEARED at script start. The file contains only THIS run's output, so an AI agent or automation tailing it sees the current lifecycle end-to-end.
do: Instantiate SysMLv2Logger with the (name, File) overload: def logger = LoggerClass.newInstance('MyScript', new File(logsDir, 'MyScript.log')).
Put the file under the project's logs/ directory (per CLAUDE.md — that's where Claude looks).
Include an opening marker line: logger.info('=== MyScript run started ===') so tails start with a clean anchor.
Always pass the Throwable to logger.error(msg, t) — the stack trace is what the agent reads to root-cause.
dont: Don't append to a shared log file across runs — mixed output makes it impossible to know which lines came from this run.
Don't System.exit or Runtime.halt on error — let the JVM keep running and let the file buffer flush.
Don't put the log file inside the Cameo install dir — those trees are often read-only and get wiped by upgrades.

verify-fqn.summary: MANDATORY: call javadoc_verify_fqn on every com.nomagic.* / com.dassault_systemes.* FQN BEFORE emitting it as an import. FQN hallucinations are the #1 failure mode — package paths like '.classes.mdprofiles' look plausible but don't exist. The verify tool returns the correct FQN as a candidate when yours is wrong.
do: For every import line you are about to write, call javadoc_verify_fqn first. If exists=false, use the first candidate from the response.
Prefer helpers and factories in javadoc_search results — they are boosted by the ranker; ecore/impl/emfuml2xmi are demoted because they are plumbing, not the scripting surface.
Cross-check fluent-builder idioms against the real examples with example_search — e.g. 'StereotypesHelper.addStereotype' as one literal string. Real code beats inferred signatures.
When in doubt, pass BOTH simple name and package to javadoc_search with kind='class' and read the top 3 FQNs.
dont: Never emit an FQN you did not verify. 'It looks like the other ones in that package' is how classes.mdprofiles.Stereotype gets hallucinated — the real FQN is mdprofiles.Stereotype.
Don't assume nested packages follow a pattern just because a sibling did. UML2 metamodel packaging is irregular (some metaclasses live directly under ext.magicdraw, others under ext.magicdraw.<pkg>).
Don't pick ecore / impl / emfuml2xmi classes as the primary reference — they are internal wrappers and often differ from the public API.

batch-runner.summary: For the 'ultimate script testing tool', run your task in Cameo's batch mode by extending com.nomagic.magicdraw.commandline.ProjectCommandLine (or CommandLineAction for plugin-scoped tasks). Cameo launches, executes your main(), and exits — providing real classpath-bound validation.
do: Extend ProjectCommandLine for tasks that operate on a project; implement execute(Properties, Project).
Implement CommandLineAction for tasks that rely on a plugin already loaded into the running app.
Launch from the Cameo install root: java -cp lib/classpath.jar com.example.MyRunner <args>. The bundled JRE is at bin/vm.options; use the JAVA_HOME from bin/mcse.properties.
Always return a byte exit code — 0 for success, non-zero for failure. Let the caller (CI, script, Claude) read the code.
Print to printToCommandLineConsole() for user-visible output, and MDLog.getGeneralLog() for diagnostic output — both end up in the application log.
Even in batch mode, the application requires a graphical environment (per guide 254437299). A headless VM will not work.
dont: Don't System.exit — ProjectCommandLine.launch() handles exit for you; manual exit skips Cameo's shutdown hooks.
Don't assume the plugin classpath is available to a ProjectCommandLine — plugins are loaded lazily. Use CommandLineAction if you need plugin classes.
batch-runner.source: examples/imagegenerator/.../ExportDiagramImages.java; guide 254437299 (Running programs in batch mode); guide 254437340 (Implementing command line launchers); guide 254437301 (Specifying batch mode program classpath)
